narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this tough news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 45% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which was the key reason your application was rejected—they use this prediction to prioritize students likely to succeed long-term. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 4, which is below the typical student's 5.71; a strikingly low Year 3 decile of 1 compared to the average 5.53; an LSAT score of 32 versus the usual 36.7; an undergraduate GPA of 3.0 against the average 3.21; upper-middle family income, which aligns around the dataset's midpoint; and your status as a female Asian student planning full-time enrollment, both of which are less common but not drastically so (females make up 43.6%, Asians 5.9%, and full-time is the norm at 92.7%). When the model evaluated you, it weighed these factors to forecast your bar performance, and several stood out as the strongest influences. The biggest driver was your Year 3 decile—it had the most sway and pushed the prediction toward failure because it's so much lower than what succeeds for most students. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile, also contributing significantly toward the failure risk due to being below average. Your LSAT score ranked third in influence, further nudging the model toward expecting a struggle on the bar, given how it trails the strong scores of typical passers. Family income came next, playing a moderate role in heightening the predicted risk in your case. Your racial background as Asian followed, adding to the failure-leaning prediction relative to the mostly White dataset. Your undergraduate GPA had a bit less pull but still tipped things negatively compared to peers. Being full-time and female had the smallest impacts, barely shifting the needle but still aligning with the overall concern. In short, your academic deciles and test scores were the top-ranked factors pulling hardest toward failure, while others played supporting roles—unlike typical admitted students whose stronger early and late grades, plus higher LSATs, push predictions safely toward passing. To boost your future bar exam odds, focus on what you can control now: build disciplined study habits mirroring top performers, invest in targeted bar prep courses to overcome your past grade trends, consider retaking the LSAT if it boosts your profile for other schools, and seek mentorship from successful bar takers to address these academic signals head-on. With effort in these areas, you can rewrite your story and prove the model wrong. Hang in there—you've got potential, and this is just one data point.
